content.text: Every language model has a hard ceiling on how much text it can process in a single call. Understanding how that ceiling is measured—in tokens, not characters—and building systems that track and enforce budgets against it is one of the most practical skills in agentic AI engineering. Without token awareness, agents crash with API errors, incur unexpected costs, or silently truncate the context they depend on.
What it is

Tokenization is the process by which a model's vocabulary maps raw text to integer IDs before the neural network sees any data. A token is the model's atomic unit of text—not a character, not a word, but a sub-word fragment determined by a Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) or Unigram LM compression algorithm applied to a training corpus. The word 'unbelievable' might become ['un', 'believ', 'able'] (three tokens), while 'cat' is one token.

Token counting is the act of running that same tokenizer offline—before sending a request—to determine exactly how many tokens a given string (or structured message list) will consume. Token budgeting is the practice of maintaining per-component token counts and enforcing policy decisions (truncate, summarize, reject) so that the total prompt never exceeds the model's context window, and that a sufficient reserve remains for the generated response.

The vocabulary and encoding rules differ per model family. GPT-4o uses the 'o200k_base' encoding; GPT-4 Turbo uses 'cl100k_base'; older GPT-3.5 models use 'p50k_base'. Claude and Gemini have their own proprietary tokenizers. This means token counts are model-specific and cannot be reused across providers.
Why it matters

Character length is a poor proxy for token count. A single JSON object with deeply nested keys can contain far more tokens than its character count suggests, because every punctuation mark, quote, colon, and space is a separate token. Code in languages like Python tokenizes densely (keywords, indentation whitespace, punctuation all count), while dense natural-language prose tokenizes more efficiently. Agents that estimate context usage from character counts will routinely overshoot or undershoot the model's limit.

For single-turn chatbots the stakes are low—the user retypes their question. For autonomous agents the stakes are high: a multi-step workflow may collect tool outputs across dozens of steps before a final synthesis call. If that synthesis call overflows the context window, the entire run fails. Budgeting before each step catches the problem when there is still room to truncate, summarize, or switch to a model with a larger window.

Cost control is the other motivation. Prompt tokens are billed at the model's input price (often $0.003–$15 per million tokens), and cached tokens at a discounted rate. An agent that blindly passes every previous tool result back into context can multiply its prompt size by 5–10x over a long session. Token budgeting is the mechanism that keeps cost-per-run predictable.
Benefits
Pre-flight budget checks catch overflow before the API call, avoiding 400-range errors mid-run.
Per-component accounting (system / tools / history / RAG) reveals where tokens are being spent, enabling targeted optimizations.
Dynamic retrieval sizing lets RAG pipelines pack exactly as many chunks as the remaining budget allows—no guesswork.
Cost predictability: an agent that enforces a token budget produces a consistent cost per call regardless of session length.
Graceful degradation: the system can choose to summarize, evict old history, or use a smaller model rather than crashing.
CI/CD integration: automated token-count checks prevent system prompts from silently growing past thresholds across deployments.
Model routing: when a prompt exceeds a cheap model's window, the budget manager can transparently route to a larger context model.
Trade-offs

Counting tokens adds latency. Running tiktoken or a Hugging Face tokenizer on large inputs adds milliseconds of CPU time per call. For high-throughput pipelines processing thousands of requests per second, this overhead can accumulate. The API-based count_tokens endpoint (Anthropic, OpenAI) is exact but incurs a network round-trip.

No universal tokenizer. Every model family uses a different vocabulary. Maintaining separate tokenizer instances for GPT, Claude, Gemini, and open-weight models adds dependency weight and maintenance burden. When switching models mid-project, all token estimates must be recalibrated.

Approximate counts are sometimes unavoidable. When using the API's count_tokens endpoint, the counts reflect the server's tokenization, which is always accurate—but you pay one extra request. Local tokenizers like tiktoken can differ from the server's actual behavior in edge cases (special characters, unusual Unicode, very long tokens). A safety margin of 5–10% should always be applied.
Tool schema overhead is often underestimated: every tool definition is serialized to JSON and included in the prompt, consuming hundreds to thousands of tokens before any user content is added.
Counting with the wrong tokenizer gives a false sense of safety—the actual token count seen by the model may be higher.
Overly aggressive eviction of history context can hurt answer quality: the model loses important earlier turns.
How it works
Byte-Pair Encoding

The most common tokenization algorithm is Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE). During training, the algorithm starts with individual bytes and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a new vocabulary entry until it reaches a target vocabulary size (typically 32 000–200 000 tokens). The merge table is saved alongside the model. At inference, the same merge rules are applied left-to-right to convert input text into token IDs.
Given a vocabulary
and a string of
characters, the BPE encoding runs in
time in the worst case, but practical tokenizer libraries cache merge rules in a trie, reducing this to effectively
for typical inputs.
Chat template overhead

When a model processes a structured conversation (system + user + assistant turns), the API wraps each message in a chat template—additional control tokens that delineate roles. For OpenAI's ChatML format, each message adds approximately 3–4 overhead tokens. For Anthropic's Claude, the role wrapping adds a similar overhead. These overhead tokens are included in the input_tokens value returned by the API but are not counted if you only tokenize the text content itself.
Token budget decomposition
A well-structured agent decomposes its token budget into reserved slices:

attrs.latex: T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{system}} + T_{\text{tools}} + T_{\text{history}} + T_{\text{rag}} + T_{\text{user}} + T_{\text{reserve}}

content.text: where
is held back for the model's generated response. A typical configuration sets
to 10–25% of the total window. The remaining available budget is:
The dynamic parts—history, RAG chunks, and new user input—compete for
. When the sum exceeds it, the agent must evict older turns or summarize.
Architecture

The diagram below shows a token-aware agent's counting and budgeting pipeline. Each input source contributes its token count to a central counter, which reports to the budget manager. The budget manager applies policy zones—send as-is, trim, or compress—before the request reaches the LLM API.

attrs.alt: Token counting and budget management flow diagram

content.text: The key architectural insight is that counting happens before the API call, not after. By the time the request leaves the application, the budget manager has already verified it will fit within the model's context window and that sufficient headroom exists for the expected response length.
When to use it

Token counting is non-negotiable in any system where the total prompt can grow dynamically: agentic pipelines with tool use, RAG systems that inject retrieved documents, multi-turn chat applications with long conversation histories, and batch-processing pipelines that concatenate multiple documents.
Before inserting tool results: tool outputs can be arbitrarily large (a database query might return thousands of rows); always count before appending.
When sizing RAG chunks: count how many tokens each retrieved chunk consumes and stop retrieving once the budget is exhausted.
In CI/CD pipelines: add a test that counts the system prompt and all tool schemas; fail the build if the total exceeds a threshold.
When switching models: recount all prompts with the new model's tokenizer—the same text can differ by 15–30% between model families.
For cost estimation: count before each API call and accumulate per-session totals to generate cost reports.
In streaming agents: track running token counts per step to detect when a session is approaching the context limit.

When not to use it: for one-off scripts with short, fixed prompts where overflow is structurally impossible, adding token counting adds complexity without benefit. Simple prompt templates with bounded inputs do not need runtime counting—a static analysis at development time is sufficient.
How to implement
Step 1: Install the right tokenizer

For OpenAI models, use tiktoken. For Anthropic's Claude, use the SDK's count_tokens API endpoint. For Hugging Face open-weight models, use the model's own tokenizer loaded through the transformers library.

# OpenAI / tiktoken
pip install tiktoken

# Anthropic SDK (also includes count_tokens support)
pip install anthropic>=0.25.0

# Hugging Face (open-weight models)
pip install transformers
Step 2: Build a TokenBudget class
Centralize all token arithmetic in a single module so that the retrieval pipeline and the orchestration loop share the same budget state.
Step 3: Integrate into the agent loop

budget = TokenBudget(model="gpt-4o", context_limit=128_000, output_reserve=4096)

# Fixed components (counted once at startup)
budget.reserve("system", system_prompt)
budget.reserve("tools", json.dumps(tool_schemas))

# Dynamic components (recounted each loop iteration)
budget.reserve("history", format_messages(conversation_history))

# Decide how many RAG chunks to include
chunks_used = []
for chunk in retrieved_chunks:
    if budget.available - budget.count(chunk) < 1000:  # keep 1k safety buffer
        break
    budget.reserve(f"chunk_{len(chunks_used)}", chunk)
    chunks_used.append(chunk)

print(budget.breakdown())
# {'components': {'system': 340, 'tools': 1820, 'history': 4110, 'chunk_0': 512, ...},
#  'used': 8670, 'available': 115330, 'utilization_pct': 7.0, 'status': 'OK'}
Examples
Example 1 — tiktoken basics (OpenAI)
A minimal demonstration of counting tokens with tiktoken for the GPT-4o model family.

import tiktoken

# Load the encoding for the target model
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")

texts = {
    "short English sentence":  "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.",
    "Python code snippet":     "def fib(n):\n    return n if n < 2 else fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)",
    "JSON object":             '{"role": "user", "content": "Hello, world!"}',
    "repeated whitespace":     "   " * 100,
}

for label, text in texts.items():
    tokens = enc.encode(text)
    print(f"{label:<30} chars={len(text):>5}  tokens={len(tokens):>4}  ratio={len(text)/len(tokens):.1f}ch/tok")

# Sample output:
# short English sentence         chars=   44  tokens=   9  ratio=4.9ch/tok
# Python code snippet            chars=   58  tokens=  19  ratio=3.1ch/tok
# JSON object                    chars=   36  tokens=  11  ratio=3.3ch/tok
# repeated whitespace            chars=  300  tokens= 100  ratio=3.0ch/tok
Example 2 — Anthropic count_tokens API
Anthropic exposes a dedicated count_tokens endpoint that uses the server-side tokenizer. This is the most accurate method for Claude models and includes tool schema overhead.

import os
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic(api_key=os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"])

# Define a realistic system prompt and tool
system = "You are a senior software engineer. Answer concisely and accurately."

tools = [{
    "name": "search_codebase",
    "description": "Search the codebase for a given pattern.",
    "input_schema": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "pattern": {"type": "string", "description": "Regex or literal pattern to search for."},
            "path":    {"type": "string", "description": "Directory path to restrict the search."},
        },
        "required": ["pattern"],
    }
}]

messages = [
    {"role": "user", "content": "How do I implement a binary search tree in Python?"},
]

# Count tokens BEFORE sending the real request
count = client.messages.count_tokens(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    system=system,
    tools=tools,
    messages=messages,
)

print(f"Input tokens (incl. system + tools): {count.input_tokens}")
# Input tokens (incl. system + tools): 387

# Only send if we have budget
CONTEXT_LIMIT = 200_000
OUTPUT_RESERVE = 8_000

if count.input_tokens + OUTPUT_RESERVE < CONTEXT_LIMIT:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=OUTPUT_RESERVE,
        system=system,
        tools=tools,
        messages=messages,
    )
    print(f"Actual input_tokens billed: {response.usage.input_tokens}")
    print(f"Output tokens billed:       {response.usage.output_tokens}")
else:
    print("Budget exceeded — truncate or summarize before sending.")
Real-world example

Consider an autonomous code-review agent that processes pull requests. Each PR consists of a system prompt (~300 tokens), 10–15 tool schemas (~2 000 tokens), up to 20 changed files (each 100–500 lines of code), and a multi-turn dialogue history if the agent has already asked clarifying questions. A large PR can easily total 60 000–90 000 tokens before any response is generated.

import tiktoken
import json

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")
CONTEXT_LIMIT = 128_000
OUTPUT_RESERVE = 4_096
SAFETY = 0.05  # 5% margin
HARD_LIMIT = int(CONTEXT_LIMIT * (1 - SAFETY)) - OUTPUT_RESERVE  # ~117_504

def count(text: str) -> int:
    return len(enc.encode(text))

def review_pr(system: str, tools: list, history: list, changed_files: list[dict]) -> dict:
    """
    Token-aware PR review orchestrator.
    changed_files: list of {'path': str, 'diff': str}
    Returns the messages list ready to send to the LLM.
    """
    used = count(system) + count(json.dumps(tools)) + 3  # chat priming
    for msg in history:
        used += count(msg["content"]) + 4  # per-message overhead

    # Greedily include files while budget allows
    included = []
    skipped = []
    for f in sorted(changed_files, key=lambda x: count(x["diff"])):  # smallest first
        diff_tokens = count(f["diff"]) + 20  # path + formatting overhead
        if used + diff_tokens > HARD_LIMIT:
            skipped.append(f["path"])
        else:
            included.append(f)
            used += diff_tokens

    print(f"Budget: {used}/{HARD_LIMIT} tokens used ({100*used/HARD_LIMIT:.1f}%)")
    print(f"Included {len(included)} files, skipped {len(skipped)}: {skipped}")

    # Build message with included files only
    file_block = "\n\n".join(
        f"### {f['path']}\n```diff\n{f['diff']}\n```" for f in included
    )
    messages = history + [{"role": "user", "content": f"Review these changes:\n{file_block}"}]
    return messages

# Usage
messages = review_pr(
    system="You are an expert code reviewer...",
    tools=[{"name": "post_review_comment", ...}],  # type: ignore
    history=[],
    changed_files=[
        {"path": "src/auth.py",   "diff": "... 200 lines ..."},
        {"path": "tests/test_auth.py", "diff": "... 150 lines ..."},
        # ... more files
    ]
)

By sorting files smallest-first, the agent includes as many files as possible within the budget. Skipped files are listed in the response so the human reviewer knows which files need manual inspection. The total latency cost of running tiktoken on all diffs is typically under 50 ms even for large PRs.
Common mistakes
Using the wrong tokenizer

The most frequent error is using tiktoken's cl100k_base encoding to count tokens for a Claude or Llama model. Because each model has a different vocabulary, the same string can differ by 10–30% in token count across tokenizers. The correct approach is always to use the official tokenizer for the target model.

# WRONG: using GPT tokenizer for a non-GPT model
import tiktoken
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")
n = len(enc.encode(my_claude_prompt))  # may undercount by 20%+

# RIGHT: use the API's count_tokens endpoint for Claude
count = client.messages.count_tokens(model="claude-opus-4-5", messages=[...])
n = count.input_tokens
Ignoring tool schema tokens

Tool schemas are serialized as JSON and included in every request, consuming prompt tokens even when no tool is invoked. Ten tool definitions with detailed parameter descriptions can easily total 3 000–6 000 tokens. Agents that count only the message content miss this fixed overhead.

# WRONG: counting messages only
total = sum(count(msg["content"]) for msg in messages)

# RIGHT: also count tool schemas
tool_tokens = count(json.dumps(tools))
total = tool_tokens + sum(count(msg["content"]) for msg in messages)
Forgetting per-message formatting overhead

Each message in a chat-format request is wrapped by the model's chat template with role delimiters and special tokens. For OpenAI models this adds roughly 4 tokens per message plus 2 conversation-priming tokens. Ignoring this overhead causes off-by-N errors that accumulate in long conversations.
Counting only the content of messages, not the chat template overhead, leads to context-window overflows in long conversations.
Using character-count heuristics (e.g. '4 chars per token') instead of the actual tokenizer produces estimates that can be off by 2x for code or JSON.
Not reserving output budget: setting max_tokens=4096 without subtracting it from the available context budget can cause overflow when the prompt is near the limit.
Caching token counts for mutable inputs: if a retrieved document or history message changes between agent steps, the cached count is stale.
Best practices
Maintain a single shared TokenBudget instance per agent session. Never scatter token-counting calls across the codebase.

Always count with the same tokenizer the target model uses. For proprietary models (Claude, Gemini), use the vendor's count API; for open-weight models, load the model's tokenizer from the model card.
Reserve at least 10% of the context window as a safety margin to absorb tokenizer approximation errors and unexpected overhead.
Count tool schemas at startup and treat them as a fixed overhead that is subtracted from the available budget before dynamic content is considered.
When evicting history, remove the oldest non-essential turns first. Preserve the system prompt, the most recent user message, and any turns that anchor the agent's current task.
Log a per-call breakdown (system, tools, history, RAG, user) so that cost spikes are attributable to a specific component.
Add a CI test that counts the system prompt and all tool schemas after each change. Block merges that push the fixed overhead past a configured threshold.
Use cached context features (Anthropic prompt caching, OpenAI prompt caching) for stable prefix content. Count the cached prefix separately so cost calculations are accurate.
Performance & scalability

Local tokenizer libraries (tiktoken, Hugging Face tokenizers) are implemented in Rust or C and run on the order of 1–5 ms for inputs up to 10 000 tokens. For high-throughput pipelines processing tens of thousands of documents per second, this overhead is non-trivial. Two strategies mitigate it.
First, batch counting: tiktoken's encode_batch function encodes multiple strings in a single call with parallelism across CPU cores, reducing per-string overhead significantly for bulk operations.

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")
# Batch encode 1 000 documents in parallel
documents = ["document text..." for _ in range(1_000)]
token_lists = enc.encode_batch(documents, num_threads=8)
counts = [len(t) for t in token_lists]

Second, count caching: for content that does not change between requests (system prompts, tool schemas, static RAG documents), cache the token count at load time and reuse it. Only count dynamic content (user messages, freshly retrieved chunks) per call.

At scale, the token counting overhead is dominated by the network latency of the LLM call itself (50–500 ms), making local counting effectively free. The API-based count_tokens endpoint adds 20–100 ms of network latency and should only be used when absolute accuracy is required (e.g., final pre-flight check before a high-cost call).
Tooling & ecosystem
tiktoken (OpenAI)

tiktoken is the official tokenizer library for all OpenAI models. It loads BPE merge rules from a CDN on first use (then caches locally) and exposes a fast Rust-backed encode/decode API. It is the reference implementation for GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o, and GPT-4o-mini.

import tiktoken

# List available encodings
print(tiktoken.list_encoding_names())  # ['gpt2', 'r50k_base', 'p50k_base', 'cl100k_base', 'o200k_base']

# Get encoding by model name (resolves to correct encoding automatically)
enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4o")  # -> o200k_base
print(enc.n_vocab)  # 200019

# Inspect tokens for debugging
tokens = enc.encode("Hello, world!")
print(tokens)         # [9906, 11, 1917, 0]
print(enc.decode(tokens))  # 'Hello, world!'
Anthropic SDK count_tokens

The Anthropic Python SDK exposes client.messages.count_tokens(), which sends a lightweight request to the API and returns the exact token count the server would use. It accepts the same parameters as messages.create (system, tools, messages) and returns a TokenCountResponse.
Hugging Face tokenizers
For open-weight models (Llama, Mistral, Mixtral, Phi, Qwen), load the tokenizer from the model's Hugging Face repository. The tokenizers library is also Rust-backed and fast.

from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")
ids = tok.encode("Hello, Llama!")
print(len(ids))  # token count
LangChain token counting utilities

LangChain provides get_num_tokens_from_messages() helpers that wrap the underlying tokenizer and account for chat template overhead. They are convenient for projects already using LangChain's chain abstractions, though they introduce a dependency and may lag behind model updates.
Comparison with alternatives
Several strategies exist for managing context size. The table below compares token counting and budgeting against simpler alternatives.
Approach
Accuracy
Latency overhead
Works across models
Best for
Token counting (local)
High (≈model's count)
1–5 ms (CPU)
No — per-model tokenizer
High-throughput pipelines; pre-flight checks
Token counting (API)
Exact
20–100 ms (network)
Yes — vendor handles it
Final safety check; billing-accurate estimates

Yes
Quick back-of-envelope estimates only

Yes
Non-code English text only
Fixed chunk size (e.g. 512 chars)
None
None
Yes
Offline document chunking with no dynamic content
Max-len truncation at N chars
None
None
Yes
Simple chatbots with bounded prompts; not for agents

Local token counting is the right default for agent orchestration: it is fast enough to run inline, accurate to within a few percent, and adds negligible overhead compared to the LLM call latency. The API-based endpoint is the right choice when billing accuracy matters or when the tokenizer for a new model is not yet available locally.
Security & reliability

Adversarial prompt injection can deliberately inflate token counts. If an agent accepts untrusted text as part of a tool result or user message, an attacker can craft a response that fills the remaining context window, crowding out system instructions or history. A token budget that logs per-component counts makes this class of attack visible—a sudden spike in 'tool_result' tokens should trigger an alert or rejection.

Context overflow is a reliability failure mode. If the budget check is bypassed—for example, in a fast path that skips counting for speed—the API will return a 400 ContextLengthExceeded error. Autonomous agents must treat this as a fatal step failure with a retry policy: truncate history, re-count, and retry the call. Without a retry handler, the agent silently aborts the task.
Never trust user-controlled content to be 'short': always count it, not estimate it.
Validate budget status before every LLM call, not only at session start—dynamic content accumulates across steps.
Set a per-session maximum token budget (e.g. 500 000 total input tokens) to limit cost exposure if the agent enters an infinite tool-calling loop.
Monitor the ratio of tool-schema tokens to content tokens: a ratio > 0.5 indicates the tool registry is too large and should be pruned or paginated.
Use structured logging (JSON log lines) for token breakdowns so that dashboards and alerts can track cost per step, per session, and per user.
Mental model

Think of the context window as a whiteboard with a fixed surface area. The system prompt and tool schemas are printed at the top in permanent ink—they are always there and always consume the same space. The conversation history and retrieved documents are written in erasable marker—they grow as the session proceeds and must be erased (evicted or summarized) when the whiteboard is full. The token counter is simply a ruler that measures how much space each section occupies. The budget manager is the person holding the eraser, deciding what to remove before writing the next entry.

The crucial shift from character-length thinking to token thinking is recognizing that different types of text have different densities. Dense JSON and code fill the whiteboard faster than natural language prose. Knowing the density of each component—measured in tokens, not characters—is what enables precise control.
Key takeaways
Tokens are the billing and capacity unit for every LLM API—characters are irrelevant to context limits.
Always use the tokenizer that matches the target model: tiktoken for OpenAI, the vendor API for Claude, the model's own tokenizer for open-weight models.
Count tokens before sending the request, not after. Post-call usage fields confirm billing but cannot prevent overflow.
Tool schemas, per-message chat-template overhead, and formatting all consume tokens and must be included in budget calculations.
Decompose the budget into named components (system, tools, history, RAG, user) to identify which part is driving cost or overflow.
Apply a 5–10% safety margin below the hard context limit to absorb tokenizer approximation errors and unexpected overhead.
Cache counts for stable content (system prompts, static tool schemas) and only count dynamic content on each request.
Log per-component token breakdowns as structured data to enable cost attribution, anomaly detection, and budget regression alerts in CI.
Adversarial inputs can deliberately exhaust the context window—validate and cap token counts for all untrusted content before inserting it into the prompt.
